turns.content: I'm stuck on my side project and I have no idea why the compiler is screaming at me with errors when I try to use generics in a method chain. It feels like it's just ignoring my type hints completely because of how collect() is chained.

That frustration is super common for hobbyists when they first hit turbofish syntax in Rust. The real reason this happens is that the compiler can't deduce which generic parameter you want to apply because method chains are ambiguous without explicit disambiguation. When you write something like vec().into_iter() without specifying the type, the compiler doesn't know if you're targeting a specific lifetime or a different generic bound in a complex trait implementation, so it throws an error until you add the turbofish syntax explicitly.
Okay, but I tried adding ::<T> after collect() and it still didn't work. Is there something wrong with how I'm structuring my function signature?

The issue is likely that you're trying to use a turbofish on a method call inside a generic context where the type isn't fully resolved yet, which confuses the compiler's type checker. You need to make sure your function accepts an explicit argument or uses a trait object if you want flexibility. Also, I'm curious—what exactly are you collecting into? Are you building a Vec<T> or maybe a HashMap<String, T>?
I am building a Vec<T>, but I keep forgetting to write ::<T> before collect(). It seems like the turbofish is required every time in chains.

That's exactly right. In Rust, whenever you chain methods that involve generics or lifetimes without an explicit type annotation, the compiler demands a turbofish to resolve the ambiguity for you. For example, if you have .collect::<Vec<_>(), the ::<T> tells the compiler precisely which generic parameter to bind at that point in the expression tree. Once you add that disambiguation, your method chain compiles cleanly without needing extra type hints elsewhere.
